coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQD) from Archidendron bubalinum pods and its compatibility for voltammetric detection of cadmium ion
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Setyorini, Dian Ayu
Serunting, Muhamad Allan
Sri Rizki, Wa Ode
Mufti, Nandang
Saraswaty, Vienna
Aziz, Muhammad
Suryana, Risa
Septina, Wilman
Zulhadjri,
Setiyanto, Henry
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQD) have been acknowledged as novel carbon-based materials with distinctive optical properties and excellent performance for detecting cadmium ion (Cd2+). Herein we report a facile synthesis of CQD from a natural source that is Archidendron bubalinum pods (CQDAb) and its application for an electroanalysis of Cd2+. The synthesized CQDAb was found emitting a strong blue fluorescence. Moreover, the synthesized CQDAb showed lower band gap (4.04 eV) than that of CQD synthesized from graphite (6.09 eV). For electroanalysis purposes, the CQDAb was used as a modifier for carbon paste electrode-based reduced-graphene oxide (CPE/r-GO/CQDAb). The composite of rGO/CQDAb could enhance the performance of CPE/CQDAb and CPE/rGO. The prepared CPE/rGO/CQDAb shows a greater linearity for Cd2+ detection when tested using a square wave voltammetry method. These findings clearly show promising results for preparing CQD-based natural source, specifically A. bubalinum pods, and its application for a Cd2+ sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Biomass
Hydrothermal synthesis
Voltametric detection
Cadmium sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV–visible and fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) characterization of carbon quantum dots
Electrodeposition of CQDAb on CPE surface
Electrodeposition of rGO on CPE surface
Electrodeposition of rGO/CQDAb on CPE surface
Modification of CPE with CQDAb, rGO, and rGO/CQDAb
Study of voltammetric detection of Cd with CPE and modified CPE

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thanked Analytical Chemistry and Biochemistry Research Group, Chemistry Department, Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences, Insititut Teknologi Bandung for supporting this research. The authors also thanked Riset Kolaborasi Indonesia (RKI) 2024 (1841/IT1.B07.1/TA.00/2024) for funding this research.

The authors thanked Analytical and Biochemistry Research Group, Chemistry Department, Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences, Insititut Teknologi Bandung for supporting this research. The authors also thanked Riset Kolaborasi Indonesia (RKI) 2024 (1841/IT1.B07.1/TA.00/2024) for funding this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The color of A. bubalinum pods extract (A), CQD from graphite (B), and CQD from A. bubalinum pods; (b) UV lamp irradiation of A. bubalinum pods extract (A), CQD from graphite (B), and CQD from A. bubalinum pods (C); (c) UV–Visible spectra of A. bubalinum pods extract, CQD from graphite, and A. bubalinum pods; (d) band gap of CQD from A. bubalinum pods; (e) band gap of CQD from graphite. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Electrodeposition of (a) CQDAb, (b) rGO, and (c) rGO/CQDAb on CPE surface in 0.1 M K2SO4 in a potential window of −1.0 V–1.0 V and a scan rate of 100 mV/s.
FTIR spectra of Kabau pods extract, CPE, CQDAb, and CPE/CQDAb.

The dependence of scan rate and square-root of scan rate on the peaks current of 0.01 M K3[Fe(CN6)] and K4[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M NaCl for (a–b) CPE/CQDAb and (c–d) CPE/rGO, and (e–f) CPE/rGO/CQDAb. Scan rates were varied from 20 to 200 mV/s.
(a) Square Wave Voltammeograms of Cd ions reduction on the working electrode surface, namely (a) CPE/CQD, (b) CPE/rGO, and (d) CPE/rGO/CQD in 0.1 M NaNO3 and scan rate of 20 mV/s.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQD) from Archidendron bubalinum pods and its compatibility for voltammetric detection of cadmium ion

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQD) have been acknowledged as novel carbon-based materials with distinctive optical properties and excellent performance for detecting cadmium ion (Cd2+). Herein we report a facile synthesis of CQD from a natural source that is Archidendron bubalinum pods (CQDAb) and its application for an electroanalysis of Cd2+. The synthesized CQDAb was found emitting a strong blue fluorescence. Moreover, the synthesized CQDAb showed lower band gap (4.04 eV) than that of CQD synthesized from graphite (6.09 eV). For electroanalysis purposes, the CQDAb was used as a modifier for carbon paste electrode-based reduced-graphene oxide (CPE/r-GO/CQDAb). The composite of rGO/CQDAb could enhance the performance of CPE/CQDAb and CPE/rGO. The prepared CPE/rGO/CQDAb shows a greater linearity for Cd2+ detection when tested using a square wave voltammetry method. These findings clearly show promising results for preparing CQD-based natural source, specifically A. bubalinum pods, and its application for a Cd2+ sensor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sustainable research has received considerable attention among the researchers, especially in chemistry, involving the materials' source, synthesis process, and analysis method. This is because the term of green chemistry meets the environmental and economic goals at the same time [1]. Among the research findings of green chemistry investigations, research on carbon quantum dots (CQD) attracts great attention because of their nature that is easy to produce, low-cost, considerably safe, and can be synthesized from a biomass of agriculture [2,3].

CQD is 0-dimensional material with good optical characteristics and photocatalyst [4,5]. This material promotes high surface area, photostability, low toxicity, and supercapacitor properties [6,7]. CQD is primarily synthesized using a rich carbon source such as glucose, citric acid, tapioca, graphite, as well as biomass [2,8–12]. CQD is acknowledged as an effective modifier in sensor development for analysis and monitoring of inorganic and organic compounds, as well as microorganisms, including metals [13–15], bisphenol A [16], mycobacterium [17], organophosphate [8], etc. The CQD-based sensor has been developed due to its fluorescent ability. The potential of CQD in sensor development is due to the CQD's fluorescent ability, where the concentration of an analyte can be quantitatively measured according to the photoluminescence intensity. For example, Tachapermpon et al. developed a biosensor based CQD from citric acid to monitor formaldehyde [18]. Meanwhile, Ma et al. have developed a copper ion sensor from CQD of peanut shells [12].

Although CQD-based sensors have been developed, the use of CQD for electroanalysis is still limited. Thus, this present study aimed to synthesize CQD from A. bubalinum pods (CQDAb) using a two-step hydrothermal process and investigated its compatibility for the detection of Cd2+ electrochemically.

The materials used in this study included analytical grades of chemicals reagents, such as CdCl2.2H2O (Merck), NaCl (Merck), K2SO4 (Merck), NaNO3 (Merck), K3[Fe(CN6)] (Merck), and K4[Fe(CN)6] (Merck). The solutions were prepared using demineralized water. The A. bubalinum pods were obtained from a local market in Ulumusi, South Sumatera, Indonesia. The A. bubalinum pods were cleaned, then dried at room temperature. The dried pods were then crushed into powder. The A. bubalinum pods powder was then stored in a plastic bag prior to investigations. Meanwhile, rGO was obtained from ITNano, Medan, North Sumatera, Indonesia.

CQDAb was synthesized using a two-step hydrothermal synthesis according to the protocol: about 5 g of A. bubalinum pods powder was weighed and placed into a beaker glass. Subsequently, ethanol solution (20 % v/v) was added to the beaker glass. The mixture was continuously stirred at a temperature of 80 °C. The heating process was performed until the volume of solution was reduced by approximately 50 %. Next, the mixture was transferred into the PTFE tube. The PTFE tube was placed in an oven and heated at 185 °C for 6 h. After, the PTFE was cooled to room temperature. The reaction product was then washed with 100 mL of demineralized water and transferred into a centrifuge tube. The supernatant was separated from agglomerated particles by means of centrifugation (6000 rpm, 15 min). The CQDAb was obtained by filtering the supernatant using a syringe filter with a pore size of 0.22 μm. As a comparison, a CQD was prepared using a similar protocol with graphite, and the carbon source was also synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–visible and fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) characterization of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The success of CQD synthesis was demonstrated by the fluorescence light emitted from the materials when irradiated under UV lamp (312 nm) in a black box. The maximum absorbance of the synthesized CQDs and A. bubalinum pods extract was observed using a UV–vis spectrophotometer at a wavelength ranging from 200 to 800 nm. The surface functional groups of as-synthesized CQD, A. bubalinum pods extract, carbon paste, and carbon paste modified with CQDAb were characterized using an FTIR spectrophotometer (Prestige 21, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodeposition of CQDAb on CPE surface
ce:section.ce:para: About 3 mL of as synthesized CQDAb was diluted using 0.1 M K2SO4 to a total volume of 10 mL in a volummetric flask. The diluted CQDAb solution was then transferred into an electrochemical cell using a 3-electrode system (working electrode, Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and Pt wire as the auxiliary electrode). The electrodeposition process was performed using a cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique at a potential window from −1.0 to 1.0 V, for 25 cycles, and at a scan rate of 100 mV/s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodeposition of rGO on CPE surface
ce:section.ce:para: About 3 mg of rGO was dispersed in 10 mL of 0.1 M K2SO4 and was sonicated for 10 min. The dispersed rGO was electrodeposited on the CPE surface using CV technique at a potential window of −1.0 to 1.0 V, for 25 cycles and at a scan rate of 100 mV/s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodeposition of rGO/CQDAb on CPE surface
ce:section.ce:para: The composite of rGO-CQDAb was electrodeposited on the surface of CPE using the CV technique for 25 cycles. Prior to electrodeposition, about 3 mg of rGO was mixed with 3 mL of CQDAb. The mixture was diluted using 0.1 M K2SO4 until the total volume of 10 mL. Subsequently, the mixture was sonicated for 10 min for homogenization. The dispersed composite (rGO-CQDAb) was then electrodeposited on CPE surface at a potential window ranging from −1.0 to 1.0 V, at scan rate of 100 mV/s.

The electrochemical performances of CPE and modified CPEs were assessed using a standard method. Those electrodes were primarily studied for the adsorption-diffusion effect on the redox reaction of 0.01 M Fe(CN)6 3/4- in 0.1 M NaCl. The reference electrode was Ag/AgCl (3 M NaCl), while the auxiliary electrode was Pt wire. Subsequently, CPE and modified CPEs were used as the working electrode to measure Cd ions in 0.1 M NaNO3 and a scan rate of 20 mV/s using Square Wave Voltammetry (SWV) from −0.2 to −1.1 V.

In this investigation, CQD was synthesized using A. bubalinum pods as a carbon precursor. The synthesis involved a two-step hydrothermal process, where the first stage is carbon extraction and pre-concentration of carbon precursor, and the second step is the formation of carbon dots. As shown in Fig. 1 a–A, at the first stage, the A. bubalinum pods extract depicts a brownish color. The CQD prepared from graphite resulted in a colorless solution (see Fig. 1a–B). Whereas the CQDAb shows a yellow transparent color (see Fig. a–c). When the solutions in Fig. 1a were further observed under UV irradiation in a black box, the CQDAb emitted a strong blue fluorescence (see Fig. 1b–C).

In contrast with CQD from graphite, no blue fluorescence was emitted (see Fig. 1b–B). This phenomenon can be explained as follows: the CQD from graphite seems to have fewer functional groups on its surface, unless heavily oxidized or chemically modified. The surface functional groups play an important role in creating surface states contributing to blue fluorescence. The CQDAb likely contains natural organic compounds originating from A. bubalinum pods extract, such as carbohydrates, proteins, or flavonoids, which can act as carbon precursors as well as provide surface functional groups [19]. Those functional groups from A. bubalinum pods extract enhance the blue fluorescence and improve the water solubility of CQDAb. As the impact increased, the more blue fluorescence was emitted from CQDAb, providing a more transparent solution.

Next, the optical characteristics of CQDAb was investigated. As shown in Fig. 1c, the UV–visible spectrum of CQDAb has a distinct peak at 280 nm, whereas the peak of A. bubalinum pods extract was found at a wavelength of 274 nm with a lower and broader peak than that of CQDAb.). This CQD can be classified as C-CQD. The peaks at positions of 280 and 274 nm are typical characteristics that correspond to an aromatic π−π∗ transition of the C=C bond and are related to electron transitions in oxygen-containing CQD [6,20,21]. Those peaks are suggested from bioactive molecules from A. bubalinum pods extract. It is very interesting to note that CQD from graphite does not have a peak band, implying that fewer functional groups are present on the CQD surface from graphite. Doubtless, resulting in a larger band gap due to surface passivation (see Fig. 1d–e).

This finding contrasts with the previous study conducted by Devi et al. who reported a CQD produced from graphite rod also emitted fluorescence [22]. The difference can be due to the sources of graphite and the steps to produce CQD. Generally, carbon sources can be transformed into CQD because of their structure with sp2/sp3 [23]. But, the hydrothermal method in this present study could not turn graphite powder into CQD due to the low energy and insufficient temperature used. Incorporating the fluorescence investigation, UV–Vis analysis, and band gap calculation, CQD from graphite was excluded in the following investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of CPE with CQDAb, rGO, and rGO/CQDAb
ce:section.ce:para: Modification of carbon paste electrode (CPE) using CQDAb, rGO, and rGO/CQDAb was performed electrochemically. The modifiers were electrodeposited on the surface of CPE using the CV technique for 25 cycles at a potential window of −1.0 to 1.0 V and a scan rate of 100 mV/s in 0.1 M K2SO4. This method will provide a strong interaction between the modifiers and the CPE surface and offer uniform distribution compared to drop casting method [24,25]. Fig. 2 a–c depict the cyclic voltammogram of CPE when electrodeposited with CQDAb, rGO, and rGO/CQDAb, respectively. As shown, all of the modified CPEs showed a similar trend as suggested by the decrease in current responses. The cyclic voltammogram of CPE modified with CQDAb (Fig. 2a) shows the presence of visible redox peaks, indicating that the surface functional groups of CQDAb contribute to electron transport. A little sharp anodic peak at 0.059 V and a broad peak at 0.549 V began to appear at cycle-12 and gradually grew until cycle-25, showing some substance experienced oxidation and then layered the CPE surface. A reduction peak was also observed at 0.049 V and disappeared at cycle-20. Those redox peaks originated from C=O and C-OH contained in CQDAb [22,27], which introduced CQD's debonding and bonding properties [26]. The redox peaks also support the rechargeable properties of the electrode [27].

The cyclic voltammogram of rGO modified CPE shows broader redox peaks compared to CQDAb modified CPE, suggesting a double-layer capacitance. An anodic peak was found at 0.349 V that shifted to the more positive potential following the increase of cycles (Fig. 2b). It also had a cathodic peak at 0.099 V, showing a redox couple reaction in the electrodeposition process. The double layer structure of rGO can hinder mass transfer when it forms a compact coating on the CPE surface, then reducing active surface accessibility. The decrease in peak current showed rGO layer had been formed.

When the rGO/CQDAb-composite modified CPE, the cyclic voltammogram depicts narrower and less distinct peaks (see Fig. 2c), suggesting a good synergistic effect between CQDAb and rGO. It seems the CQDAb enhances the redox activity, while the rGO improves the electrical conductivity and provides a robust matrix for CQDAb.

FTIR characterization was used to observe the functional groups of the materials used in this study. Fig. 3 demonstrates the FTIR spectra of CPE, A. bubalinum pods extract, CQDAb, and CPE/CQDAb. The CPE spectrum shows peaks at positions of 2926, 2846, 1458, and 1373 cm−1. The weak peaks at 2926 and 2846 cm−1 correspond to C-H stretching vibrations, while the peaks at 1458 and 1373 cm−1 are likely due to C=C vibration of the aromatic ring [28]. The FTIR spectrum of CPE provides a baseline for comparison with modified CPE. Since CQDAb was prepared from A. bubalinum pods extract, it is necessary to analyze the functional groups originating from the extract. As shown, A. bubalinum pods extract shows a broad peak at positions 2308 and 2382 cm−1 corresponding to C≡N groups. Whereas the peaks at 1637 and 1384 cm−1 could be attributed to C=O and C-H groups, respectively [21,29]. The presence of functional groups in A. bubalinum pods extract indicates that the extract contains a mixture of organic compounds, which can act as reducing and stabilizing agents in the synthesis of CQDAb. The FTIR spectrum of CQDAb clearly shows a reduction in % transmittance for peaks at positions 1637 and 1384 cm−1, indicating the involvement of those functional groups in the synthesis of CQDAb. When the CQDAb was electrodeposited on the surface of CPE, a new peak at 1087 cm−1 was found to be present in the spectrum of CPE/CQDAb corresponds to the C-O-C or C-OH group, which may arise from CQDAb. In addition, the peaks at 2927 and 2852 cm−1 are more pronounced, indicating the integration of CQDAb with CPE.

Before the voltammetric application of CPE modified with CQDAb, rGO, and rGO/CQDAb, the electrode surface was studied to investigate the diffusion or adsorption effect. The investigation was conducted using Fe(CN)6 3 - /4- that are known to have reversible reactions and a fast electron transfer process. The increase of scan rates gradually increased the anodic and cathodic peaks. The scan rates applied were varied from 20 to 200 mV/s. The results are presented in Fig. 4 . The oxidation peak current response increased as the scan rate was raised. The simultaneous linearity in both scan rate (v) and the square root of scan rate (v 1/2) suggests that both the adsorption and diffusion are involved in the process. However, the linear relationship (R2 values) between ΔIp vs. v1/2 is closer to 1 [30] than ΔIp vs. v, demonstrating that diffusion dominantly controls electro-redox reactions. The results aligned with the logarithmic correlation between the square root of the scan rate and the current peak (see S1). The ideal slope for the curve should be 0.5 to ensure the diffusion-controlled process. But, the slopes for those three modified electrodes were far from 0.5, indicating that the process also involved adsorption. In this case, the adsorption of reactants or products on the electrode surface could occur parallelly with the electron transfer [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of voltammetric detection of Cd with CPE and modified CPE
ce:section.ce:para: The modified CPEs (CPE/rGO, CPE/CQDAb, and CPE/rGO/CQDAb) were then used for the detection of Cd2+ at concentrations ranging from 0.48 to 2.00 ppm. The cathodic peak increased following the increase of Cd ions concentration (Fig. 5 a). As shown in Fig. 5, all electrodes exhibited excellent linearity (R2 > 0.97) for reliable quantification of Cd2+ within the tested concentration range. The slopes of calibration curves represent the sensitivity of each electrode. As shown, the slopes for each electrode were 6.1672 μA/ppm, 7.3739 μA/ppm, and 7.3064 μA/ppm for CPE/CQDAb, CPE/rGO, and CPE/rGO/CQDAb, respectively. The data show that the CPE/rGO had the highest sensitivity, followed by CPE/rGO/CQDAb and CPE/CQDAb. The sensitivity of the composite electrode (CPE/rGO/CQDAb) was lower than that of CPE/rGO; this may be attributed to the shielding or blocking effect of CQDAb. The excessive or improper integration of CQDAb on the rGO matrix might partially block the active site of rGO [31,32]. This blocking effect could reduce the overall electron transfer efficiency and hinder the Cd2+ adsorption, leading to a lower sensitivity [33,34].

Meanwhile, the presence of CQDAb could enhance the linearity (R2) of CPE/rGO, from 0.9891 to 0.9915. At the concentration of 2.00 ppm, CPE/rGO surface started to saturate due to the adsorption of the reduction product/species. rGO has porous that can adsorbed product/species [33,34]. Compositing rGO with CQDAb could subtract the adsorption of the product on the electrode surface. CQDAb provides active sites containing functional groups that allow the analytes to interact with the electrode surface, which tends to have an electrostatic interaction than physical interaction (adsorption) instead, considering Cd has a positive charge and CQDAb has negative charges from the organic functional groups [29]. Additionally, from the calibration curves in Fig. 5b–d, CPE/rGO/CQDAb had LOD of 0.22 ppm, while CPE/CQDAb and CPE/rGO obtained 0.17 and 0.25 ppm, respectively. These findings demonstrated that R2 value of CPE/rGO/CQDAb was higher than CPE/rGO, and the slope of CPE/rGO/CQDAb was higher than CPE/CQDAb, suggesting that the CPE/rGO/CQDAb has the synergistic advantages of CQDAb and rGO.

This study demonstrated the characteristics of CQD synthesized from A. bubalinum pods and graphite through a two-step hydrothermal process. CQD synthesized from A. bubalinum pods has met the characteristics of general CQD and can be used as a CPE modifier through electrodeposition using the CV technique. The mechanism on the CPE modified with rGO and CQDAb was dominantly controlled by diffusion. CPE/CQDAb, CPE/rGO, and CPE/rGO/CQDAb can be used for the electroanalysis of Cd2+ using SWV. This shows the potential of A. bubalinum pods as a precursor for a facile synthesis of CQD and enabling its application for the detection of Cd2+. Although the combination of CQDAb with rGO is expected to provide a synergistic effect, the actual interaction between the rGO and CQDAb depends on their ratio, dispersion, and bonding. The further research is required to optimize the rGO to CQDAb ratio to ensure that CQDAb could enhance the functionality without excessive rGO. In addition, it is also important to ensure a uniform distribution of CQDAb on the surface of rGO during synthesis to prevent aggregation and maximize the synergistic effect.

Dian Ayu Setyorini: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Muhamad Allan Serunting: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Wa Ode Sri Rizki: Investigation, Data curation. Nandang Mufti: Validation, Methodology. Vienna Saraswaty: Writing – review & editing. Muhammad Aziz: Visualization, Formal analysis. Risa Suryana: Formal analysis, Data curation. Wilman Septina: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Zulhadjri: Software. Henry Setiyanto: Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thanked Analytical Chemistry and Biochemistry Research Group, Chemistry Department, Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences, Insititut Teknologi Bandung for supporting this research. The authors also thanked Riset Kolaborasi Indonesia (RKI) 2024 (1841/IT1.B07.1/TA.00/2024) for funding this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.vacuum.2025.114023.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Anastas, N. Eghbali, Green Chemistry: Principles and Practice, Chem Soc Rev 39 (2010) 301-312. https://doi.org/10.1039/b918763b.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal synthesis of carbon quantum dots with size tunability via heterogeneous nucleation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Nammahachak, K.K. Aup-Ngoen, P. Asanithi, M. Horpratum, S. Chuangchote, S. Ratanaphan, W.
